rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THE JAMES B. MCCLATCHY TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 15, 1974
rulings.Calendar Matter: The Mcclatchy Co. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Amended And Supplemental Petition And Complaint For Declaratory Relief, Breach Of Trust, Aiding And Abetting Breach Of Trust, And Fraud
rulings.Rulings: The McClatchy Company's ("the Company") Motion to Quash Service of Summons and Amended and Supplemental Petition and Complaint for Declaratory Relief is GRANTED. Carlos McClatchy served the Company with summons of the Amended and Supplemental Petition and Complaint for Declaratory Relief in its capacity as an alleged insurer of the Estate of William Coblentz and the Estate of Ellery McClatchy ("the Estates"). It is the service of this summons that the Company seeks to quash. While it is true that the Court already has personal jurisdiction over the Company as an individual corporate defendant, it does not have jurisdiction over the Company in its capacity as a representative of the Estates, and the Company properly utilized the motion to quash procedure set forth in C.C.P. § 418.10(a)(1) to contest service of the sumons at issue. Probate Code § 552(a) provides that in pursuing an action against a decedent protected by insurance, the action shall name as the defendant, "Estate of (name of decedent)" and summons of the action shall be served "on a person designated in writing by the insurer or, if none, on the insurer." The Company's agreement to indemnify William Coblentz and Ellery McClatchy as "director[s], officer[s], employee[s], agent[s], or fiduciar[ies]" does not render it an insurer for the purposes of service under Probate Code §552. The reasoning of Chambi v. Regents of the University of California (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 822 stands irrespective of whether Carlos seeks to pursue William Coblentz and Ellery McClatchy as trustees or employees. The indemnification agreement does not create an insurer-insured relationship between those parties (or their estates) and the Company. As in Chambi, there is no contract whereby, for consideration paid to the Company, Ellery's and Coblentz's liability (as trustees or otherwise) shifted to the Company. (Id. at 827.) Service of the summons under section 552 on the Company as an "insurer" of the Estates was therefore improper. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:30 p.m. Tuesday, October 27, 2015.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THE JAMES B. MCCLATCHY TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 15, 1974
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer Of The Estate Of William Coblentz, Deceased, To The Amended And Supplemental Petition And Complaint And The First Amendment Thereto; And Notice Of Its Motion To Strike Punitive Damage Allegations (Only Re: The Coblentz Law Firms Lawyers Professional Liability Insurance Policy)
rulings.Rulings: The Demurrer of the Estate of William Coblentz ("the Estate") (sued pursuant to Probate Code § 550, 552) to Carlos McClatchy's Amended and Supplemental Petition and Complaint ("the FAP") and First Amendment thereto is OVERRULED in part and SUSTAINED in part. The Motion to Strike Punitive Damage Allegations is GRANTED. The demurrer is overruled as to Counts Two through Five and Seven, the non-fraud, breach of trust claims. The Court cannot determine on demurrer that the statute of limitations set forth in C.C.P. § 340.6 bars those claims in their entirety as it is not clear based on the pleadings before the Court that they depend solely on proof that the insured, William Coblentz, violated a professional obligation, i.e., one owed "by virtue of being an attorney." (Lee v. Hanley (2015) 61 Cal.4th 1225, 1236-1237.) Under the circumstances of this case, the Court cannot resolve the affirmative defense of laches on demurrer. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the claim for declaratory relief in Count One and the claim for fraud and fraudulent concealment in Count Eight of the FAP, and the motion to strike the prayer for punitive damages as to the Estate is granted. Carlos did not respond to the Estate's arguments regarding those claims in his Opposition. Carlos can recover against the Estate only to the extent of any insurance coverage. With respect to Count One, the policy does not extend to claims for declaratory relief and, in any event, the Estate will not be governed by any declaration the Court makes with respect to the rights and obligations arising under the Shareholders' Agreement, to which it is not a party. With respect to Count Eight, fraud and fraudulent concealment is not liability for which the decedent is covered by insurance. (See Probate Code § 550(a); Insurance Code § 533.) Similarly, the policy would not cover an award of punitive damages, public policy would bar such coverage, and the underlying conduct alleged in support of punitive damages cannot be pleaded as to the Estate. Because Carlos only alleged Count Ten of the FAC (aiding and abetting breach of trust) against to the Coblentz Law Firm, which is no longer a party to this action, that claim does not extend to the Estate, and is not before the Court on this demurrer. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:30 p.m. Tuesday, October 27, 2015.
